mnemonics.title: E-Waste Categories
mnemonics.content: TIPS: Temperature exchange, Pumps, IT equipment, Screens, Large appliances, Small appliances - the 6 categories under WEEE Directive

mnemonics.title: Hazardous E-Waste Elements
mnemonics.content: LEAD (Lead, Mercury, Arsenic, Brominated flame retardants) + CADMIUM - Remember: Lethal Elements Cause Dangerous Adverse Reactions

mnemonics.title: E-Waste Statistics
mnemonics.content: 62 million tonnes by 2022, 17% recycled - Remember: 62/17 = divide by 3.5 roughly for global average

mnemonics.title: Top E-Waste Countries
mnemonics.content: CHINA USA INDIA JAPAN GERMANY - C-U-I-J-G (See You In Japan, Germany)

mnemonics.title: E-Waste Management Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: R-R-R: Reduce first, Reuse second, Recycle third - Priority order for environmental management

acronyms.usage: Policy making producers financially responsible for end-of-life product management
EU directive setting e-waste collection and recycling targets
EU directive restricting toxic materials in electronics
UN agency publishing Global E-waste Monitor
Older display technology containing lead and other hazardous materials
Component containing valuable precious metals like gold and copper

visualTips.visualization: Create a pie chart showing Asia (50%), Americas (25%), Europe (20%), Africa (5%) - visualize the world map weighted by e-waste volume with Asia being the largest slice
Draw an inverted pyramid: Prevention (top, smallest), Reuse (next), Recycling (larger), Energy Recovery (larger), Disposal (bottom, largest but least desirable)
Draw arrows from developed countries (USA, EU, Japan) pointing to developing countries (China, India, Africa), with Basel Convention as a barrier trying to stop illegal flows
Draw a cross-section of a computer showing lead in solder/cathode rays, mercury in switches/backlights, cadmium in batteries, brominated flame retardants in plastics

analogies.analogy: Like panning for gold in river streams, but instead of rivers, we 'pan' through discarded electronics to extract precious metals - our cities become mines for secondary resources
If we landfill e-waste, we bury valuable resources - urban mining is like having a goldmine in our garbage dumps that we're not exploiting
It's like wealthy households sending their trash to neighbors' backyards and pretending it's a 'gift' - the Basel Convention tries to prevent this digital dumping
It's like only recycling 1 out of 6 plastic bottles - with 17% formal recycling, we're losing 5 out of every 6 units of valuable materials
E-waste is growing faster than the world population - like a financial investment with compound interest working against environmental sustainability
